author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XXIII. 14.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Woe unto you, Scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye devour widows’ houses, and for a pretense make long prayers: therefore ye shall receive greater damnation.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, next He derides them for gluttony: and the grievous thing was, that not from rich men’s goods, but from the poor they indulged their own belly, and aggravated their poverty, which they should have relieved.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither did they merely eat, but devoured.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Moreover also the manner of their overreaching was yet more grievous, “for a pretense making long prayers.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For every one is worthy of vengeance who doeth any evil thing; but he that is deriving even the reason for so doing from godliness, and is using this cloke for his wickedness, is justly liable to a far more grievous punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore did He not depose them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because the time suffered it not as yet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So therefore He lets them alone for a time, but by His sayings, He secures that the people be not deceived, lest, through the dignity of those men, they be drawn on to the same emulation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as He had said, “Whatsoever they bid you do, that do;” He shows how many things they do amiss, lest from thence He should be supposed amongst the unwise to commit all to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Woe unto you, for ye shut up the kingdom against men; for ye neither go in yourselves, neither suffer ye them that are entering to go in.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if to profit no one be a charge against a man, even to hurt and hinder, what plea hath that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what means, “them that are entering in?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Them that are fit for it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when they were to lay injunctions on others, they used to make the burdens intolerable, but when they themselves were to do any of the things required, on the contrary, so far from doing anything, they went much beyond this in wickedness, they even used to corrupt others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These are they that are called pests,who make their employment the ruin of others, standing right contrary to teachers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it be the part of a teacher to save that which is perishing, to destroy that which is on the point of being saved is that of a destroyer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, again another charge: “Ye compass sea and land to make one proselyte, and when he is made, ye make him twofold more the child of hell than yourselves;”that is, not even the fact that hardly ye have taken him, and with endless toils, induces you to be sparing towards him, although of the things we have hardly acquired, we are more sparing, but you not even this renders more gentle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here He lays to their charge two things; one, that they are unprofitable for the salvation of the many, and need much toil in order to win over even one; another, that they were remiss in the preservation of him whom they had gained, or rather that they were not only careless, but even traitors, by their wickedness in their life corrupting him, and making him worse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when the disciple sees his teachers to be such as these, he becomes worse than they.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he stops not at his teacher’s wickedness; but as when his teacher is virtuous, he imitates him, so when he is bad, he even goes beyond him, by reason of our proneness to what is evil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He calls him “a child of hell,” that is, a very hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He said “twofold more than you,” that He might both alarm those, and make these feel the more severely, because they are teachers of wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not this only, but because they labor to instill into their disciples a greater wickedness, hardening them to a much greater depravity than they have, and this is above all a mark of a depraved soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then He derides them for folly also, because they bade them disregard the greater commandments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet before He had said the opposite, that “they bind heavy burdens, and grievous to be borne.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things too they did again and were doing everything for the corruption of those who were subject to them, in little things requiring strictness, and despising the great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For ye pay tithe,” He saith, “of mint and anise, and have omittedthe weightier matters of the law, judgment, and mercy, and faith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These ought ye to have done, and not to leave the others undone.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here then He naturally saith it, where it is tithe and almsgiving, for what doth it hurt to give alms?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not to keep the law; for neither doth it say thus.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore here indeed He saith, “These ought ye to have done;” but where He is speaking about clean and unclean, He no longer adds this, but makes a distinction, and shows that the inward purity is necessarily followed by the outward, but the converse is no longer so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For where there is a plea of love to man, He passes it over lightly, for this very reason, and because it was not yet time expressly and plainly to revoke the things of the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But where it is an observance of bodily purification, He overthrows it more plainly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So, therefore, while with respect to alms He saith, “These ought ye to have done, and not to leave the others undone,” touching purifications He speaks not on this wise, but what?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Ye make clean,” He saith, “the outside of the cup and the platter, but within they are full of extortion, and injustice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Cleanse that which is within the cup, that the outside may be clean also.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He took it from a thing confessed and manifest, from a cup and platter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, to show that there is no harm arising from despising bodily cleansings, but very great vengeance from not regarding the purifications of the soul, which is virtue, He called these “a gnat,” for they are small and nothing, but those other a camel, for they were beyond what men could bear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He saith, “Straining at the gnat, and swallowing the camel.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed the one were enacted for the sake of the other, I mean of mercy and judgment; so that not even then did they profit being done alone.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas the little things were mentioned for the sake of the great, and after that these last were neglected, and labor was spent on those alone, nothing was gained even then by this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the greater followed not the lesser, but the lesser were sure to follow these greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things He saith to show, that even before grace was come, these were not among the principal things, or amongst those upon which men should spend their labor, but the matters required were different.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if before the grace they were so, much more when high commandments had come, were these things unprofitable, and it was not meet to practise them at all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In every case then is vice a grievous thing, but especially when it does not so much as think it needs amendment; and it is yet more grievous, when it thinks itself sufficient even to amend others; to express which Christ calls them “blind guides.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if for a blind man not to think he needs a guide be extreme misery and wretchedness; when he wishes himself to guide others, see to what a gulf it leads.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things He said, by all intimating their mad desire of glory, and their exceeding frenzy concerning this pest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this became a cause to them of all their evils, namely, that they did all things for display.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This both led them away from the faith, and caused them to neglect what really is virtue, and induced them to busy themselves about bodily purifyings only, neglecting the purifications of the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So therefore to lead them into what really is virtue, and to the purifyings of the soul, He makes mention of mercy, and judgment, and faith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these are the things that comprise our life, these are what purify the soul, justice, love to man, truth; the one inclining us to pardonand not suffering us to be excessively severe and unforgiving to them that sin (for then shall we gain doubly, both becoming kind to man, and hence meeting also ourselves with much kindness from the God of all), and causing us both to sympathize with them that are despitefully entreated, and to assist them; the other not suffering them to be deceitful, and crafty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But neither when He saith, “These ought ye to have done, and not to leave the others undone,” doth He say it as introducing a legal observance; away with the thought;neither with regard to the platter and the cup, when He said, “Cleanse that which is within the cup and platter, that the outside of them may be clean also,” doth He bring us unto the old regard for little things, but on the contrary indeed, He doth all things to show it to be superfluous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He said not, Cleanse the outside of them also, but that which is within, and the outside is sure to follow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, neither is it concerning a cup and platter he is speaking, but of soul and body, by the outside meaning the body, by the inside the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if with regard to the platter there be need of that which is within much more with regard to thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But ye do the contrary, saith He, observing things trifling and external, ye neglect what are great and inward: whence very great mischief arises, for that thinking ye have duly performed all, ye despise the other things; and despising them, ye do not so much as strive or attempt to perform them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, He again derides them for vainglory, calling them “whited sepulchers,”and unto all adding, “ye hypocrites;” which thing is the cause of all their evils, and the origin of their ruin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He did not merely call them whited sepulchers, but said, that they were full of uncleanness and hypocrisy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things He spake, indicating the cause wherefore they did not believe, because they were full of hypocrisy and iniquity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things not Christ only, but the prophets also constantly lay to their charge, that they spoil, that their rulers judge not according to the rule of justice, and every where you may find the sacrifices indeed refused, but these things required.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that there is nothing strange, nothing new, neither in the lawgiving, nor in the accusation, nay not even in the comparison of the sepulchre.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the prophet makes mention thereof, neither did he call them merely a sepulchre, “but their throat an open sepulchre.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Such are many men now also, decking themselves indeed outwardly, but full of iniquity within.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For now too there is many a mode, and many a care for outward purifications, but of those in the soul not so much as one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if indeed any one should tear open each man’s conscience, many worms and much corruption would he find, and an ill savor beyond utterance; unreasonable and wicked lusts I mean, which are more unclean than worms.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that “they” should be such persons is not “so” dreadful a thing (although it be dreadful), but that “you,” that have been counted worthy to become temples of God, should of a sudden have become sepulchers, having as much ill savor, this is extreme wretchedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He in whom Christ dwells, and the Holy Spirit hath worked, and such great mysteries, that this man should be a sepulchre, what wretchedness is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What mournings and lamentations doth this call for, when the members of Christ have become a tomb of uncleanness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider how thou wast born, of what things thou hast been counted worthy, what manner of garment thou hast received, how thou wast built a temple without a breach! how fair! not adorned with gold, neither with pearls, but with the spirit that is more precious than these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider that no sepulchre is made in a city, so then neither shalt thou be able to appear in the city above.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if here this is forbidden, much more there.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather even here thou art an object of scorn to all, bearing about a dead soul, and not to be scorned only, but also to be shunned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me, if any one were to go round, bearing about a dead body, would not all have rushed away? would not all have fled?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Think this now likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thou goest about, bearing a spectacle far more grievous than this, a soul deadened by sins, a soul paralyzed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Who now will pity such a one?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when thou dost not pity thine own soul, how shall another pity him that is so cruel, such an enemy to himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If any one, where thou didst sleep and eat, had buried a dead body, what wouldest thou not have done? but thou art burying a dead soul, not where thou dinest, nor where thou sleepest, but in the members of Christ: and art thou not afraid lest a thousand lightnings and thunderbolts be hurled from above upon thine head?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how dost thou even dare to set foot in the churches of God, and in holy temples, having within thee the savor of so much abomination?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if one bearing a dead body into the king’s courts and burying it would have suffered the utmost punishment, thou setting thy foot in the sacred courts, and filling the house with so much ill savor, consider what a punishment thou wilt undergo.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Imitate that harlot who anointed with ointment the feet of Christ, and filled the whole house with the odor, the opposite to which thou doest to His house!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what though thou be not sensible of the ill savor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this most of all is the grievous part of the disease; wherefore also thou art incurably diseased, and more grievously than they that are maimed in their bodies, and become fetid.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that disease indeed is both felt by the sick and is without any blame, nay even is deserving of pity; but this of hatred and punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since then both in this respect it is more grievous, and from the sick not being sensi ble of it as he ought to be; come, give thyself to my words, that I may teach thee plainly the mischief of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But first listen to what thou sayest in the Psalm, “Let my prayer be set forth in Thy sight as incense.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then not incense, but a stinking smoke arises from thee, and from thy deeds, what punishment dost thou not deserve to undergo?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is the stinking smoke?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Many come in gazing about at the beauty of women; others curious about the blooming youth of boys.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, dost thou not marvel, how bolts are not launched, and all things are not plucked up from their foundations?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For worthy both of thunderbolts and hell are the things that are done; but God, who is long-suffering, and of great mercy, forbears awhile His wrath, calling thee to repentance and amendment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What doest thou, O man?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Art thou curiously looking after women’s beauty, and dost thou not shudder at thus doing despite unto the temple of God?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Doth the church seem to thee a brothel, and less honorable than the market-place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in a market-place indeed thou art afraid and ashamed to appear to be looking after any woman, but in God’s temple, when God Himself is discoursing unto thee, and threatening about these things, thou art committing whoredom and adultery at the very time in which thou art being told not to do this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And dost thou not shudder, nor stand amazed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These things do the spectacles of wantonness teach you, the pest that is so hard to put down, the deleterious sorceries, the grievous snares of the thoughtless, the pleasurable destruction of the unchaste.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore the prophet also blaming thee, said, “Thine eyes are not good, neither is thine heart.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It were better for such men to be blind; it were better to be diseased, than to abuse thine eyes for these purposes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It were meet indeed that ye had within you the wall to part you from the women; but since ye are not so minded, our fathers thought it necessary by these boardsto wall you off; since I hear from the elder ones, that of old there were not so much as these partitions; “For in Christ Jesus there is neither male nor female.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in the apostle’s time also both men and women were together.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because the men were men, and the women women, but now altogether the contrary; the women have urged themselves into the manners of courtezans, but the men are in no better state than frantic horses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Heard ye not, that the men and women were gathered together in the upper room, and that congregation was worthy of the heavens?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And very reasonably.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even women then practised much self-denial, and the men gravity and chastity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear, for instance, the seller of purple saying, “If ye have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come in, and abide with me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear the women, who went about with the apostles, having taken unto themselves manly courage, Priscilla, Persis, and the rest; from whom our present women are as far removed as our men from their men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For then indeed even travelling into far countries women brought not on themselves evil report; but now even though brought up in a chamber, they hardly escape this suspicion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things arise from their decking of themselves, and their luxury.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then the business of those women was to spread the word; but now to appear beauteous, and fair, and comely in countenance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This is glory to them, this salvation; but of lofty and great works they do not even dream.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What woman exerts herself to make her husband better? what man hath taken to himself this care to amend his wife?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is not one: but the woman’s whole study is upon the care of ornaments of gold, and raiment, and the other adornments of the person, and how to increase their substance; but the man’s both this, and others more than this, all however worldly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Who, when about to marry, inquires about the disposition and nurture of the damsel?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: No one; but straightway about money, and possessions, and measures of property of various and different kinds; like as if he were about to buy something, or to settle some common contract.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore they do even so call marriage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For I have heard many say, such a man has contracted with such a woman, that is, has married.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they offer insult to the gifts of God, and as though buying and selling, so do they marry, and are given in marriage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And writings there are, requiring greater security than those about buying and selling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Learn how those of old married, and imitate them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then did they marry?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They inquired about ways of life, and morals, and virtue of the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore they had no need of writings, nor of security by parch ment and ink; for the bride’s disposition sufficed them in the place of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I therefore entreat you likewise not to seek after wealth and affluence, but a good disposition, and gentleness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seek for a pious and self-denying damsel, and these will be to thee better than countless treasures.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou seek the things of God, these others will come also; but if thou pass by those, and hasten unto these, neither will these follow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But such a man, one will say, became rich by his wife!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Art thou not ashamed of bringing forward such examples?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I had ten thousand times sooner become a poor man, as I have heard many say, than gain wealth from a wife.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what can be more unpleasing than that wealth?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What more painful than the abundance?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What more shameful than to be notorious from thence, and for it to be said by all, such a man became rich by a wife?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the domestic discomforts I pass by, all that must needs result from hence, the wife’s pride, the servility, the strifes, the reproaches of the servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “The beggar,” “the ragged one,” “the base one, and sprung of base.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why, what had he when he came in?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Do not all things belong to our mistress?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou dost not care at all about these sayings, for neither art thou a freeman.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since the parasites likewise hear worse things than these, and are not pained wherefore neither are these, but rather pride themselves in their disgrace; and when we tell them of these things, “Let me have,” saith one of them, “something pleasant and sweet, and let it choke me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Alas! the devil, what proverbs hath he brought into the world, of power to overturn the whole life of such persons.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See at least this self-same devilish and pernicious saying; of how much ruin it is full.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it means nothing else than these words, Have thou no regard to what is honorable; have thou no regard to what is just; let all those things be cast aside, seek one thing alone, pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Though the thing stifle thee, let it be thy choice; though all that meet thee spurn thee, though they smear thy face with mire, though they drive thee away as a dog, bear all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what else would swine say, if they had a voice?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What else would filthy dogs?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But perhaps not even they would have said such things, as the devil hath persuaded men to rave.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I entreat you, being conscious of the senselessness of such words as these, to flee such proverbs, and to choose out those in the Scriptures that are contrary to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what are these?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Go not,” it is said, “after thy lusts, and refrain thyself from thine appetites.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And, touching an harlot again, it is said in opposition to this proverb, “Give not heed to a bad woman: for honey droppeth from the lips of a woman that is an harlot, which, for a season, is luscious unto thy throat; but afterwards thou shalt find it more bitter than gall, and sharper than a two-edged sword.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Unto these last then let us listen, not unto those.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For hence indeed spring our mean, hence our slavish thoughts, hence men become brutes, because in everything they will follow after pleasure according to this proverb, which, even without arguments of ours, is of itself ridiculous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after one is choked, what is the gain of sweetness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Cease, therefore, to set up such great absurdity, and to kindle hell and unquenchable fire; and let us look steadfastly (at length though late) as we ought, unto the things to come, having put away the film on our eyes, that we may both pass the present life honestly, and with much reverence and godly fear, and attain unto the good things to come, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory world without end.
